mnemonics.title: Thread Direction Memory
mnemonics.content: L-thread = LEFT points to PREDECESSOR; R-thread = RIGHT points to SUCCESSOR. Remember: LP (Left Predecessor), RS (Right Successor) - both end with same letter as pointer direction!

mnemonics.title: NULL Pointer Count
mnemonics.content: n + 1 NULLs always - Think: 'n Nodes need n+1 NULLs to hold them all in line!' This is because trees connect like a linked list with one extra end pointer.

mnemonics.title: Threaded Traversal Steps
mnemonics.content: LEFTmost → VISIT → If rthread=1: follow RIGHT to successor; Else: go RIGHT child then LEFTmost. L-A-V-R-F-R-L pattern: Look At Visitor's Right Follow Right Leg!

acronyms.usage: A binary tree using NULL pointer slots as threads for efficient traversal
Flag indicating left pointer is a thread (1) or child pointer (0)
Flag indicating right pointer is a thread (1) or child pointer (0)

visualTips.visualization: Draw inorder sequence horizontally: A → B → C → D → E. For each node, draw arrows showing how NULLs become threads connecting to these inorder positions. Left thread points leftward, right thread points rightward.
Box with 5 fields: [LThread|LData|RData|RThread]. When LThread=0: LChild→LeftChild; when LThread=1: LData→Predecessor. Same logic for right side.
Think of thread flags as traffic signals: 0 = 'GO' (follow pointer to child), 1 = 'STOP' (pointer leads to thread/relative). Visualize green light for children, red light for threads.

analogies.analogy: Regular binary tree is like a family tree where empty seats (NULLs) are wasted. Threaded tree is like reusing those empty seats to store 'next in line' tickets for an event - using otherwise wasted space!
Think of threads as seat reservations. When there's no left child (empty seat), we 'reserve' that spot with a ticket pointing to who sat before (predecessor) - making queue movement efficient!

analogies.analization: lthread and rthread are like railway track switches. When switch is 0 (child direction), train goes to child. When switch is 1 (thread direction), train follows the thread line to reach the next station.
